text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic procedure for a recurrent incisional hernia using Palmer's point for entry, located at the left costal margin and midclavicular line. The abdominal wall is held, revealing the hernia boundary and rectus diastasis. A small stab wound is made, and the abdominal wall is stretched below the costal margin. A Veress needle is inserted perpendicularly towards the stomach, followed by irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests. The preset pressure is set at fifteen, with actual pressure at eight to nine, and gas flow rate increased to one liter. Once the actual pressure matches the preset pressure, the Veress needle is removed and the incision is enlarged.

The surgeon introduces a 10mm optical trocar and inserts the telescope to examine the hernia area. The baseball diamond concept is applied to position additional ports, with the telescope at 24 cm and instruments at 18 cm from the target. Three ports are planned for the abdominal wall repair.

The surgeon positions the right and left hand ports and begins the procedure, revealing a large incarceration with extensive adhesions. Using a left hand atraumatic grasper, the surgeon stretches the abdominal wall while performing adhesiolysis. The peritoneal dissection starts at the periphery of the bowel, avoiding direct contact with the bowel using the harmonic scalpel. The vibrating jaw of the harmonic scalpel is used to dissect the boundaries of the peritoneum defect, which is completely incarcerated in the abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs meticulous dissection using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques to separate the adhered bowel loops from the abdominal wall. The periphery between the medial structures and ligament is visible, showing extensive bowel adhesion.

The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac, with the left hand stretching the bowel loop downward and the right hand separating the sac peritoneum. The pre-peritoneal fat is visible, and a clear plane for separation is established. Multiple defects are observed, which will be treated as a single defect for mesh placement.

The surgeon performs aggressive dissection to separate lateral adhesions and incarcerated bowel loops. The peritoneum of the hernia sac is carefully separated from the bowel loop, with visible identification of clear boundaries before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon carefully holds the hernia sac with minimal handling of the bowel, slowly separating the sac from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon identifies another defect and trims the hernia sac to prevent traction on the bowel. The previous roll-in suture is visible, but no mesh is found, indicating it was likely not used in the prior surgery.

Approximately seventy-five percent of the fibrotic tissue has been removed, revealing a diastasis of the rectus muscles. The surgeon uses the left hand to stretch the bowel downward while the right hand employs a vibrating jaw instrument to cut fibrotic tissue over the ring of the rectus, aiming to release the bowel without causing harm.

The surgeon completes the dissection in the current area, focusing on separating the bowel loops. Below the surgical field is the bladder area, requiring careful navigation between the medial umbilical ligaments.

The surgeon inspects the bowel for perforations, finding no issues. The defect is closed using extracorporeal Mishra's knot technique, with Prolene sutures tightly approximating the rectus margins. Multiple knots are applied to ensure secure closure before placing the mesh, reducing the risk of recurrence.

The surgeon performs the extracorporeal knot tying technique, sequentially creating the first, second, and third winds and locks to securely approximate the edges of the rectus muscle. The knot is tightened to bring the tissue edges closer together, with pressure reduced to optimize the closure.

The surgeon carefully takes reverse bites to approximate the tissue margins, closing any small defects with multiple extracorporeal knots and Western knots. The medial umbilical ligaments and bladder are visible between the ligaments. A polyurethane mesh is placed with Gore-Tex sutures tied at the corners for trans-facial fixation. The mesh is secured using a titanium tacker for outer and inner crowning, taking care to avoid the inferior epigastric vessels. Slight oozing from the hernia sac is noted but controlled by mesh pressure. The procedure concludes with abdominal strapping using an elastic adhesive to prevent seroma formation.
